APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This paper clearly sits within the NLP/ML research area: it neuralizes a well-known discourse representation (the entity grid) and proposes a convolutional model over grids (Sections 2–3) which is directly relevant to NLP venues. It evaluates on standard coherence tasks (discrimination, insertion) and datasets (WSJ, DUC’03) and compares to established baselines (Barzilay & Lapata, Elsner & Charniak, graph-based methods), using expected ranking training and common optimization/regularization practices (pairwise ranking, dropout, Adam) (Sections 4–5). The problem framing, methods, and evaluations are appropriate for a top-tier NLP/ML audience and are positioned with respect to the discourse/coherence literature throughout (Related Work).

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important problem (automatic modeling of local discourse coherence) that underpins tasks like sentence ordering, summary coherence rating, essay scoring and story generation (see Sections 2 and 4). Its neuralization of the entity-grid (Grid-CNN and Extended Grid-CNN) yields consistent empirical gains (e.g., ~4% absolute in discrimination and ~1–2.5% in insertion; Section 5.3 and Table references), showing practical value over prior grid and graph models. The contribution is a useful and timely extension (convolutional modeling over entity grids, distinct from Li & Hovy 2014) that is likely to be adopted and built upon within coherence and related NLP tasks, but it is incremental rather than transformative and has limited immediate implications beyond discourse/coherence modeling.

CLARITY.justification: The paper contains clear fragments (e.g., the entity-grid description and Gi,j definition, and the experimental setup with model comparisons), but overall presentation is degraded by extensive repetition, duplicated paragraphs, and stray/fragmented sentences (multiple repeated copies of the Barzilay & Lapata description and broken lines like “Compared to discrimination, gain in insertion is less verbose.”). Main contributions are stated, but not early or crisply framed; figures/tables are referenced (Figure 2, Tables 3/4) but not shown here and their role is unclear from the text. Notation is partially defined but the manuscript shows inconsistent/garbled passages and formatting issues that hinder readability and require substantial copyediting and reorganization.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a clear new method: neuralizing the entity-grid coherence representation using a convolutional neural network (Grid-CNN and Extended Grid-CNN) and training it with pairwise ranking, which is different from prior neural approaches that operate at the sentence-level (e.g., Li & Hovy 2014). It also applies wide convolutions to capture long-range entity transitions and embeds entity-specific features into distributed representations, which is a non-trivial extension of the classic entity-grid line of work (Barzilay & Lapata, Elsner & Charniak). While the idea of applying neural nets to coherence is expected, the specific combination (CNN over entity-grid plus entity-feature embedding) is a notable and clearly differentiated contribution rather than a purely incremental reuse of existing techniques.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states clear technical claims (neural CNN over entity grids, pairwise ranking) and evaluates on standard tasks/datasets with appropriate baselines (Grid, Extended Grid, graph model, WSJ, DUC), which supports the main claims in principle. However the excerpt omits crucial experimental details and results (tables are referenced but not shown), provides no statistical significance testing, and lacks ablation/hyperparameter details (e.g. filter sizes, embedding initialization, training set sizes per experiment), so the empirical support is incomplete. There are no nontrivial mathematical derivations to check and the learning objective is standard and plausible, but the claim of state-of-the-art and the reported gains are not fully substantiated in the provided text. Overall the methodology is reasonable but missing evidence and analysis justify a rating of 3.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (Barzilay & Lapata 2008, Elsner & Charniak 2011, Guinaudeau & Strube 2013) and reports results on the same established tasks/datasets (WSJ for ordering, DUC’03 for summary rating) using standard toolkits (Brown coherence toolkit, cohere6) and metrics (discrimination/insertion accuracy and ranking) (see Sections 5.1, 5.3 and 4.2). Comparisons appear fair (same data and toolkits) and the authors quantify gains (e.g., Table 3/4 discussions) and note when they use fewer features than the Extended Grid yet still improve. Minor shortcomings: the neural model of Li & Hovy (2014) was trained but ultimately excluded from result tables despite being discussed, and a few other neural baselines are only discussed qualitatively rather than fully reported.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper evaluates on multiple standard tasks (discrimination, insertion, summary coherence rating) and datasets (WSJ, DUC'03) and compares against several baselines including extended grid and graph-based models, which supports the main claims (see Sections 4–5 and reported gains). Related work is reasonably thorough, citing key prior models (Barzilay & Lapata, Elsner & Charniak, Guinaudeau & Strube, Li & Hovy). However, ablation/sensitivity analysis is limited: although Grid-CNN vs Extended Grid-CNN and non-neural counterparts are compared, there is no per-feature ablation of the three entity-specific features or hyperparameter sensitivity; failure analysis is cursory (only a brief note on weaker insertion gains), and reproduction details are incomplete (architecture/filter sizes and many training/meta details are not provided despite mentioning initialization, dropout, and optimizers).
